exemplar.title: To investigate the relationship between Leading Questions (IV) and Reconstructive Memory of the recall of car speeds (DV)

detailedExemplarData.title: To investigate the relationship between Leading Questions (IV) and Reconstructive Memory of the recall of car speeds (DV)

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and well-explained aim with real-world relevance
Thorough description of reconstructive memory theory and link to the investigation
Fully operationalised hypotheses with explicit IV and DV definitions
weaknesses: Could add more precise operational definitions (e.g. specific participant age range) in the aim
Initial definition of memory is not explicitly tied back to reconstructive memory theory
Minor room to deepen the theoretical context further
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets the descriptor for band 5: the aim is clearly stated and its relevance is explained; the theory/model is accurately described and linked; hypotheses are operationalised. A perfect band-6 treatment would include more detailed operational definitions and tighter theoretical framing, so a score of 5 reflects high quality with minor areas for refinement.

strengths: Independent-groups design is well explained with rationale to avoid order effects
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are described and justified
weaknesses: Control variables and materials are described but lack full explanation of how each control defends against specific validity threats
Limited discussion of potential biases from opportunity sampling
No explicit comment on how choice of only two verbs affects construct validity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the design and sampling meet the âexplainedâ level, the treatment of control variables and materials is closer to mere description with partial justification. This places the work solidly in the middle band (3), reflecting good methodological awareness but incomplete depth in explaining every choice.

strengths: Accurate use of descriptive (means, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics
Correctly labelled bar graph that directly addresses the hypothesis
weaknesses: Interpretation shows some confusion over the direction of the effect
P-value reporting conventions are inconsistent (missing leading zero)
Redundant restatement of hypotheses rather than focused interpretation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The statistical analysis and graphical presentation are appropriately and accurately applied (band 5), but the interpretation of findings contains conceptual inconsistencies, placing the overall analysis in band 4. A score of 4 reflects strong technical execution with room to tighten the interpretive clarity.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to reconstructive memory theory and compared to the original study
Strengths and limitations are relevant and fully explained in relation to validity
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified
weaknesses: Depth of discussion is moderate; broader literature and mechanisms beyond the original study are not explored
A few limitation points (e.g., sample representativeness) are underdeveloped or incomplete
Suggested follow-up research could be more specific
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows clear reference to theory, well-explained strengths/limitations, and justified modifications (band 5 for two strands), but the discussion lacks depth and broader contextualisation (band 3â4 for the discussion strand). Overall, band 4 accurately captures this mixed profile.

strandSelections.reasoning: A correctly labelled bar graph displays mean speed estimates for each verb, directly targeting the hypothesis.  Axes, units and condition labels are present and appropriate, so the presentation fulfils the top-band requirements.

The student states that results are significant and links this to accepting the hypothesis, but then notes the direction is inverted, revealing some conceptual confusion.  Findings are nevertheless interpreted with reference to data and hypothesis, albeit imperfectly, suiting the 3-4 band.

Both research and null hypotheses are written; the IV is operationalised as the verbs âsmashedâ vs âcontacted,â and the DV is operationalised as estimated speed in mph.  Units and categories are explicit, so the hypothesis is fully operationalised.

Opportunity sampling is identified; participant characteristics (age, gender, non-psychology students) and the rationale for these choices (driving experience, avoiding demand characteristics) are provided, constituting an explanation rather than mere description.

Reconstructive memory theory is described (schemas, post-event information) and the Loftus & Palmer study is summarised with results.  The student explicitly states how verb intensity in questions influences reconstruction and how the present investigation tests that mechanism, fulfilling the requirement for both description and explanation of the link.

The student compares replication results to the original study and discusses reconstructive-memory theory, yet depth is moderate and does not explore wider literature or mechanisms extensively.  This is a clear discussion but not fully sophisticated, placing it in the 3-4 band.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate the effect of leading-question verbs on speed estimates â and then explains why this is educationally and societally relevant (unreliable eyewitness testimony, courtroom implications).  The explanation explicitly links the planned replication to real-world decision making, satisfying the descriptor for an explained relevance.

Multiple strengths and limitations are identified (demand characteristics, participant variables, representativeness) and each is explained in relation to the investigation, satisfying the top-band descriptor.

Controlled variables (single viewing, same video) and materials (original crash footage) are described with partial justification, but the explanation of HOW each control ensures validity is brief; this meets the lower end of the âexplainedâ band.

Suggested changes (use repeated measures, separate seating, random sampling) are explicitly linked to the weaknesses outlined and justify how they would improve validity or reliability, meeting the criteria for full justification of modifications.
The student not only describes the independent-groups design but also explains its purpose in avoiding order effects and demand characteristics, meeting the âexplainedâ level.

Means, standard deviations and an appropriate Mann-Whitney U are used for an independent-groups, ordinal-level data set.  Calculations and critical value comparison are correct; therefore both descriptive and inferential statistics are accurately applied.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear description of reconstructive memory theory and cites key studies; the linkage to schema processes is well articulated.
The summary of the Loftus & Palmer replication background is accurate and sets up the rationale for verb intensity manipulation clearly.
The stated aim and relevance are well explained, linking courtroom applications to eyewitness reliability, demonstrating sophistication in context.
The independent-groups design is explained with rationale to avoid order effects, demonstrating clear understanding of methodological choices.
Opportunity sampling and participant characteristics are well described, but consider discussing potential biases this introduces and how it affects generalizability.
The rationale for excluding psychology students is strong and directly ties to demand characteristics, enhancing the studyâs validity.
Explanation of control variables is present but could be deepened by linking each control directly to threats to validity (e.g., demand characteristics vs memory encoding).
The choice to use only two verbs from the original five is justified by sample size, but consider discussing how this affects construct validity.
The investigation aim clearly identifies IV and DV but would benefit from operational definitions here (e.g., specific verbs, participant age) to set precise parameters.
Avoid including administrative details like word counts in the body of the investigation; move these to a cover sheet or omit for clarity.
Opening definition of memory is concise but lacks connection to reconstructive memory theory; consider linking this definition explicitly to schemas and post-event information.
Attribution of the inverted effect to sample size and driving experience is plausible but could be strengthened by referencing similar findings or empirical evidence.
The strength regarding limited demand characteristics is explained clearly, linking back to independent-groups design.
The participant-variables limitation is noted but would benefit from quantifying how driving experience variance affected results and proposing a statistical control.
Sample strength is well-linked to recruitment timing and attrition risk, illustrating thoughtful methodological planning.
The ethics checklist demonstrates comprehensive coverage of participant rights and protections, contributing to methodological rigor.
Consider grouping ethical considerations with the main method section or referencing them briefly rather than listing in isolation for better flow.
Table of means and SD is clear and well-labelled, directly supporting the hypothesis about speed estimates under different verb conditions.
Rationale for using the mean is provided, but consider clarifying why median or mode were rejected for skewed or outlier data.
Justification for standard deviation is solid, showing awareness of data variability and its implications for consistency.
The bar graph is well-labelled and addresses the hypothesis directly, enhancing visual clarity of the condition differences.
The Mann-Whitney U test is appropriate for independent ordinal data; the justification contextualizes the choice well.
Report p-values with leading zero (e.g., p < .05) and explain why U < critical value inverts the hypothesis to avoid reader confusion.
Restating the research hypothesis here is redundant; focus this section on interpreting the findings rather than repeating earlier text.
Comparison of replication and original findings is well articulated, showing understanding of theoretical implications.
The unrepresentative sample weakness is identified but the truncated sentence disrupts clarity; complete this point and link directly to recruitment modifications.
Procedure strength regarding seating separation is well explained, demonstrating attention to reducing cross-participant influence.
The proximity issue is a valid limitation; strengthen this critique by suggesting how data reliability metrics would change with spatial separation.
The conclusion states a need for more research but could be improved by proposing specific follow-up studies or experimental variations.
Duplicate citation entries for the APA Dictionary appear; remove redundancy and consolidate references in the bibliography.
Citation formatting is inconsistent (e.g., numbering, DOI inclusion); apply a uniform style guide to improve professionalism.
